shortRoutes.title: Banyoles Ardennes Long
shortRoutes.description: This route is a Girona inland classics-style ride, inspired by the feel of the Ardennes Spring Classics rather than high mountain climbing. It combines constant rolling terrain, repeated medium-length climbs, rough rhythm changes, and virtually no prolonged flat sections. Compared to the coastal routes, this one is more about attritional fatigue than scenic climbing.
shortRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona to Banyoles – Fast Inland Roads. The route opens with smooth rolling terrain, wide agricultural roads, and fast pacing opportunities. This first section typically encourages overpacing and feels easier than it really is. The terrain gradually transitions toward narrower inland roads and hillier profiles around Banyoles. 2. Banyoles Region – Rhythm Changes Begin. Around the Banyoles area, the terrain becomes significantly more irregular, climbs arrive more frequently, and the roads start to resemble Ardennes-style terrain. Expect repeated 2–5 km climbs, short steep ramps, and continuous gear shifting. 3. Key Climbs & Terrain Features. The defining terrain of the route consists of endless short-to-medium climbs that are rarely extreme individually but extremely fatiguing collectively. Many climbs are likely 1–4 km long at gradients of 4–8%, occasionally steeper on village exits. 4. Ardennes-Style Punches. What makes this route difficult is the lack of sustained recovery, frequent anaerobic spikes, and continuous cadence changes. This mirrors Liège–Bastogne–Liège style terrain, Amstel Gold Race rhythm, and Girona pro training loops. 5. Descents. Descents are shorter than typical mountain routes, frequent and technical in places, and often immediately followed by another climb. This creates very little true recovery time and a constant muscular load throughout the ride.

shortRoutes.title: Banyoles Hills
shortRoutes.description: This route is a compact inland Girona climbing loop focused on the rolling hill systems around Banyoles. Compared to the longer “Banyoles Ardennes Long” route, this version is shorter and more controlled, but still delivers a surprisingly high climbing load through continuous terrain changes.
shortRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Exit – Fast & Flowing. The opening section features smooth agricultural roads, fast rolling terrain, and gentle elevation changes. This terrain encourages high speed and allows groups to establish rhythm quickly. However, climbs begin appearing sooner than expected. 2. Transition Into Banyoles Hills. As the route approaches Banyoles, roads narrow, terrain becomes increasingly irregular, and climbing frequency rises sharply. This section defines the ride with almost constant elevation change despite the absence of huge climbs. 3. Key Climbs & Terrain Features. The core terrain consists of repeated medium-length climbs, frequent short ramps, and technical rhythm changes. Typical climbs range from 1–4 km at gradients of 4–7%, with occasional steeper pitches exiting villages. These climbs are ideal for tempo work but become fatiguing due to their repetition. 4. Rocacorba Foothill Terrain. The route profile strongly suggests movement through the lower Rocacorba region or nearby inland hill systems. This terrain includes long drags, false flats, and sustained seated climbing. Even without summiting Rocacorba itself, the surrounding roads accumulate elevation rapidly. 5. Ride Character. This is not a route defined by one major climb. Instead, fatigue builds through repetition, with climbs arriving continuously and recovery windows remaining short. The difficulty comes from sustained aerobic pressure, repeated threshold efforts, and constant terrain management. 6. Descents. Descents are short, flowing, and often technical through wooded sections. Unlike major mountain descents, they rarely provide full recovery, with another climb usually beginning shortly afterward.

longRoutes.title: Coastal Route Alternative
longRoutes.description: This route is a classic Costa Brava coastal endurance ride, blending inland Girona roads with the rugged Mediterranean coastline. Compared to the other routes you shared, this one is less of a pure mountain stage and more of a high-tempo rolling coastal route with relentless short climbing.
longRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Exit – Fast Rolling Roads. The route begins with smooth inland roads leaving Girona, featuring gradual terrain that allows groups to settle and frequent false flats rather than major climbs. 2. Coastal Transition – Signature Section. The defining feature of this route is the move onto the Costa Brava coastal roads. Once near the coast, the terrain becomes continuously undulating, climbs become shorter but steeper, and recovery between efforts is minimal. This section is extremely scenic, physically deceptive, and harder than elevation totals suggest. 3. Key Climbs on the Route. This route strongly aligns with the classic Sant Grau coastal approach, typically featuring a 7–8 km climb averaging 5–6% with multiple rhythm changes. Sant Grau is famous because it links inland Girona with the coast, offers panoramic sea views higher up, and is heavily used by pro riders training around Girona. 4. Coastal Rollers (Tossa / Sant Feliu Region). After the main climb, the road constantly rises and falls. Short climbs often hit 7–10%, especially exiting coves and coastal bends. These repeated efforts accumulate fatigue rapidly. 5. Inland Return Section. Returning inland, roads become smoother and faster, with more gradual climbs and opportunities for sustained tempo riding. However, fatigue from the coast makes even mild gradients feel harder. 6. Scenery & Road Feel. Highlights include Mediterranean sea views, cliffside roads, pine forest descents, and quiet inland Catalunya villages.

longRoutes.title: Girona Coastal: St Grau & Els Àngels
longRoutes.description: This route is a classic Girona benchmark ride, combining the rugged Costa Brava coastline with two of the region’s most iconic climbs: Sant Grau d’Ardenya and Els Àngels. It is one of the most balanced Girona routes, featuring coastal terrain, sustained climbing, fast inland tempo roads, and a long endurance feel without extreme elevation.
longRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Exit – Fast Rolling Terrain. The route begins with smooth inland roads leaving Girona, gentle rollers, and an opportunity to settle into rhythm. This opening section typically feels fast and controlled, but it can encourage overpacing when riding in a group. Gradually, the terrain becomes hillier approaching the coast. 2. Coastal Transition – Costa Brava Terrain. Once near the coast, roads narrow, elevation changes become more frequent, and the route becomes rhythmically demanding. The coastal section includes repeated short climbs, technical descents, and cliffside roads with Mediterranean sea views. This terrain creates constant power fluctuations and produces fatigue faster than expected. 3. Sant Grau d’Ardenya. One of the defining climbs of Girona cycling, Sant Grau typically measures around 7–8 km with an average gradient of 5–6%. The climb is variable and rolling rather than brutally steep. Its constantly changing gradients repeatedly interrupt rhythm, while upper sections become more exposed. Sant Grau is famous for its Mediterranean views, technical approach roads, and its role linking Girona’s inland terrain with the Costa Brava coastline. It remains a staple climb for professional riders training around Girona. 4. Els Àngels. The second signature climb of the route, Els Àngels is approximately 10 km long with a steady 3–4% average gradient. Unlike Sant Grau, Els Àngels is smooth and controlled, making it ideal for sustained seated climbing and consistent pacing. The climb is iconic due to its popularity among Girona-based professionals, sheltered tree-lined sections, and suitability for tempo and threshold efforts. By the time riders reach Els Àngels, fatigue from the coastal section is already significant, making the climb feel more demanding than the numbers suggest. 5. Descents. The route includes two distinct descent styles. Coastal descents are technical, twisty, and require attention and bike handling skills, while inland descents are faster, more flowing, and better suited for recovery and speed maintenance.

longRoutes.title: Les Planes d’Hostoles → Osor → Sant Hilari Sacalm
longRoutes.description: This route is a deep inland Catalunya mountain ride centered around one of the most iconic cycling roads in the Girona region: the Osor Road and the Sant Hilari Sacalm climb system. Compared to the coastal routes, this is a far more mountainous, sustained, and rhythm-based climbing experience, featuring long uninterrupted climbs through dense forest roads with very little traffic.
longRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Exit & Volcanic Zone Approach. The opening section heads northwest toward the Garrotxa volcanic region and the Les Planes d’Hostoles area. Terrain initially consists of rolling agricultural roads, gradual elevation gain, and fast pacing opportunities. The riding here feels smooth, efficient, and highly aerobic, although the climbing load progressively builds throughout the route. 2. Osor Road – The Signature Feature. The Osor Road is one of the most famous cycling roads in the Girona region and forms the defining feature of this ride. It is characterized by long sustained ascents, smooth gradients, and continuous forest-covered climbing. The road is highly regarded because traffic is extremely low, road surface quality is excellent, and the climbing rhythm is exceptionally consistent. Unlike coastal climbs, there are very few interruptions, allowing riders to settle into sustained tempo efforts. Typical climbing profiles involve 15–25 km of gradual uphill terrain, with most gradients ranging from 3–6% and rarely becoming excessively steep. This creates a pure endurance climbing experience focused more on aerobic stress than explosive fatigue. 3. Sant Hilari Sacalm Region. The route likely reaches or passes through Sant Hilari Sacalm, one of the highest inland plateaus near Girona. The region is famous for cool forest roads and is a popular destination for summer climbing rides. Terrain characteristics include long drags, false flats, and sustained seated climbing, rewarding riders who can maintain steady pacing over extended periods. 4. Descents. Descents on this route are long, fast, and flowing. Compared to coastal descents, they are generally less technical, feature more predictable corners, and make it easier to maintain speed. This provides better recovery opportunities and supports high-speed endurance riding over long distances.

longRoutes.title: Sant Hilari Sacalm
longRoutes.description: This route is a classic inland Girona mountain endurance ride centered around the long forest climbs leading toward Sant Hilari Sacalm. Among cyclists riding around Girona, Sant Hilari is famous for endless steady climbing, quiet mountain roads, dense forest scenery, and sustained aerobic riding. This route is smoother, more rhythmic, and less explosive than many Girona climbs, focusing instead on long-duration climbing endurance. It is one of the purest “diesel climber” routes in the collection.
longRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Rolling Terrain. Leaving Girona, the route begins with fast inland roads, gentle rollers, and smooth valley terrain. This opening section feels efficient and highly aerobic, making it ideal for settling into rhythm and group riding dynamics. The roads encourage high average speed and controlled tempo pacing before elevation gradually begins accumulating and climbs become longer and more sustained. 2. Transition Into Mountain Terrain. As the route moves inland, roads narrow, forest density increases, and gradients become steadier. This section has a distinctly remote Catalunya atmosphere with a quiet endurance-riding feel. Unlike coastal terrain, climbs here are largely uninterrupted, rhythm changes are minimal, and efforts become deeply aerobic rather than explosive. 3. Sant Hilari Sacalm Climb System. The defining feature of the route is the long ascent network toward Sant Hilari Sacalm. Typical climbs are sustained and smooth, with gradients mostly ranging between 4–6% and continuous elevation gain over many kilometers. This terrain is famous because roads are exceptionally quiet, climbs are highly consistent, and riders can maintain steady tempo efforts for extended periods. 4. Why Sant Hilari Is So Popular. The Sant Hilari region is widely used for endurance training, tempo climbing blocks, Gran Fondo preparation, and stage-race aerobic conditioning. Unlike steeper Girona climbs, the gradients rarely force explosive efforts. Instead, fatigue builds gradually and deeply, creating a huge aerobic load and a highly professional training-ride atmosphere. 5. Climb Feel. The lower slopes begin gradually through rolling terrain under tree cover, with manageable gradients encouraging smooth seated climbing and steady pacing. In the middle section, climbs become more sustained, recovery sections shorten, and fatigue accumulates steadily. This rewards cadence discipline, controlled breathing, and efficient power delivery. Near the upper elevations, roads become cooler and more exposed, while climbs feel psychologically longer as cumulative fatigue builds. The upper mountain terrain feels calm, remote, and deeply immersive. 6. Environmental Feel. This route delivers one of the strongest forest-road endurance experiences in the collection. Riders can expect dense pine forests, minimal traffic, cool mountain air, and long uninterrupted climbing roads. The atmosphere feels peaceful, rhythmic, and very different from the more dramatic Costa Brava coastal riding. 7. Descents. Descents are long, flowing, and predictable. Compared to coastal descents, they are less technical overall, allow faster average speeds, and provide better recovery opportunities. This supports continuous momentum and efficient endurance pacing on the return toward Girona. 8. Overall Impression. This is one of the smoothest pacing routes and one of the best endurance-climbing experiences in the collection. The challenge is less about attacking steep gradients and more about maintaining rhythm for hours, carefully managing fatigue, and sustaining aerobic output deep into the ride. It is a route that feels significantly bigger than the raw numbers suggest and is deeply satisfying for endurance-focused climbers.

longRoutes.title: St Hilari 125
longRoutes.description: This route is a classic Girona mountain endurance ride built around the long forest ascents leading toward Sant Hilari Sacalm. Compared to other inland routes, this version is more compact than the longest mountain epics while still remaining highly climb-focused, extremely rhythmical, and designed for sustained aerobic output. It is the kind of route that looks manageable on paper but gradually wears riders down through uninterrupted climbing load, delivering a pure diesel-climber experience and a true Girona endurance-training classic.
longRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Rolling Roads. Leaving Girona, the route opens on smooth inland valley roads, gentle rollers, and fast agricultural terrain. This opening section feels efficient and fast-moving, making it ideal for group tempo riding. The early kilometers are comfortable and highly aerobic, feeling significantly easier than the route eventually becomes. 2. Transition Toward Mountain Terrain. As the route heads inland, roads narrow, forest cover increases, and elevation gain becomes more sustained. The riding atmosphere changes dramatically as traffic decreases, climbs become uninterrupted, and the terrain develops a true mountain-road character. This is where the ride quietly begins accumulating fatigue and shifts from tempo riding into sustained climbing mode. 3. Sant Hilari Sacalm Climbing Roads. The route centers around the climb systems leading toward Sant Hilari Sacalm. Typical characteristics include long steady ascents with gradients generally between 4–6% and continuous elevation gain over many kilometers. Unlike steeper Girona climbs, rhythm interruptions are minimal, cadence remains relatively constant, and riders can settle into long aerobic efforts. This terrain is highly valued by professional riders, endurance climbers, and Gran Fondo athletes because it is ideal for aerobic conditioning and sustained pacing. 4. Climbing Feel – Lower Sections. The climbing begins subtly through rolling transitions, gradual uphill drags, and forest-lined roads. The gradients rarely shock the legs but instead slowly increase muscular load over time. This opening climbing phase encourages seated climbing and smooth power delivery rather than explosive efforts. 5. Middle Mountain Terrain. As elevation builds, climbs become more sustained, recovery sections shorten, and fatigue accumulates steadily. This section rewards smooth pacing, efficient seated climbing, and controlled breathing rhythm. The riding style here is defined by pure aerobic endurance rather than repeated high-intensity surges. 6. Upper Slopes. Near the higher elevations, temperatures cool noticeably, roads feel more remote, and climbs become psychologically longer. This is where riders begin feeling cumulative fatigue deeply, making endurance efficiency and pacing discipline increasingly important. 7. Overall Ride Character. St Hilari 125 is one of the strongest examples of Girona’s smooth, rhythm-based mountain riding. The route emphasizes sustained aerobic climbing, quiet forest roads, and gradual fatigue accumulation rather than extreme gradients or explosive efforts. It rewards patience, endurance conditioning, and the ability to maintain consistent tempo over many hours in the saddle.

longRoutes.title: Susqueda Reservoir Olot Loop
longRoutes.description: This route is a deep inland Catalunya mountain endurance loop combining the dramatic roads surrounding Susqueda Reservoir with the volcanic climbing terrain around Olot. Compared to other Girona routes, this ride feels wilder, more remote, more mountainous, and more immersive. It blends long sustained forest climbs, volcanic-region rolling terrain, remote reservoir roads, and continuous aerobic fatigue, making it one of the most adventure-oriented endurance routes in the collection.
longRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Rolling Inland Roads. Leaving Girona, the route begins on smooth inland valley roads, rolling agricultural terrain, and fast aerobic sections. This opening terrain encourages high average speed, feels relatively manageable early on, and allows riders to settle into rhythm before the route gradually becomes more rugged. Over time, climbs lengthen and elevation accumulation accelerates significantly. 2. Susqueda Reservoir Region. The roads surrounding Susqueda Reservoir are among the most dramatic inland roads near Girona. This section features narrow cliffside roads, long reservoir-edge climbs, technical terrain transitions, and remote forest scenery. The riding atmosphere feels wild, isolated, and highly immersive. Unlike the smoother Girona valley roads, rhythm becomes more irregular here, climbs feel psychologically larger, and fatigue accumulates rapidly due to the constant terrain variation. 3. Susqueda Climbs. The reservoir region contains sustained forest climbs, long gradual ascents, and repeated elevation changes. Typical climbing efforts range between 4–8 km with gradients generally around 4–7%, including occasional steeper ramps when exiting reservoir sections. The climbing style is highly aerobic, continuous, and rhythm-based rather than explosively steep. 4. Olot & Volcanic Region Terrain. Approaching Olot, the route transitions into volcanic-region roads, forest-covered mountain terrain, and smoother sustained climbing. This area is famous for its quiet roads, endless rolling mountain terrain, and popularity among professional endurance riders. Climbs in this region flow naturally through the landscape, rarely becoming brutally steep, but steadily creating deep cumulative fatigue over time. 5. Ride Character. The combination of remote reservoir terrain and volcanic mountain roads gives this route a uniquely adventurous feel compared to more traditional Girona loops. Riders experience long periods of uninterrupted aerobic riding, continuous elevation change, and deep endurance fatigue without relying on excessively steep gradients. 6. Descents. Descents vary significantly throughout the route. Around Susqueda Reservoir, roads can be narrow, technical, and highly atmospheric, requiring concentration and careful pacing. In the Olot and Garrotxa regions, descents become smoother, faster, and more flowing, allowing riders to recover while maintaining strong momentum. 7. Overall Impression. This is one of the most immersive and adventurous endurance rides in the collection. The route combines remote Catalunya landscapes, volcanic-region climbing, dramatic reservoir scenery, and sustained aerobic mountain riding into a single highly rewarding experience. It rewards pacing discipline, endurance conditioning, and the ability to manage fatigue over a very long day in the saddle.

longRoutes.title: Tossa de Mar Coastal
longRoutes.description: This route is a classic Costa Brava coastal cycling experience centered around the spectacular Mediterranean roads surrounding Tossa de Mar. Among Girona cyclists, the Tossa coastal roads are legendary for endless ocean views, constant rolling terrain, technical cliffside descents, and repeated short climbing efforts. Compared to inland mountain routes, this ride is more explosive, more rhythmically chaotic, and less about sustained climbing. Instead, it focuses on repeated surges, momentum management, and handling constantly changing terrain. It is one of the purest Girona coastal classics and one of the most scenic endurance rides in the collection.
longRoutes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Inland Roads. Leaving Girona, the route begins on smooth inland valley roads, fast rolling terrain, and gentle aerobic gradients. This opening section encourages high speed, feels efficient and controlled, and often suits group riding well. Gradually, however, the terrain becomes sharper, elevation changes become more frequent, and the influence of the coastline begins to dominate the ride character. 2. Costa Brava Coastal Section. The defining feature of the route is the spectacular terrain surrounding Tossa de Mar. This section includes cliffside roads, technical descents, short repeated climbs, and continuous ocean exposure. Riding here feels dynamic, technical, and highly engaging. Unlike inland Girona routes, there is almost no sustained rhythm. Climbs constantly interrupt momentum and recovery periods remain very short throughout the coastal section. 3. Coastal Rollers. The Costa Brava terrain creates endless short climbs, frequent steep ramps, and constant cadence changes. Typical climbs range between 1–3 km with gradients often reaching 6–10%. Descents are usually short and immediately followed by additional climbing. This creates high normalized power demands, significant muscular fatigue, and constant transitions between aerobic and threshold-level efforts. 4. Sant Grau Region. The route likely includes or approaches terrain associated with Sant Grau d’Ardenya, one of Girona’s most iconic coastal climbs. Sant Grau typically features a 7–8 km ascent with variable gradients and repeated rhythm disruptions throughout the climb. The climb is famous because it perfectly links inland Girona terrain with the Mediterranean coastline while offering exceptional scenery and constantly evolving road character. 5. Descents. Coastal descents throughout the route are technical, twisty, and highly engaging. Riders must manage braking, cornering, and rapid terrain transitions carefully. Unlike inland mountain descents, these roads rarely allow complete recovery because another climb often begins immediately after the descent ends. 6. Ride Character. This route is defined by constant momentum changes, repeated accelerations, and the challenge of maintaining rhythm on highly irregular terrain. It rewards riders who can handle repeated power surges, efficient climbing transitions, and technical descending while managing fatigue over a long endurance ride. 7. Overall Impression. Tossa de Mar Coastal is one of the purest Costa Brava cycling experiences in the collection. Combining dramatic Mediterranean scenery, rolling coastal roads, and relentless terrain changes, it delivers a uniquely engaging endurance ride that feels harder than the raw elevation numbers suggest.
